arguments.title: The Climate Pledge: Amazon's Commitment to Net-Zero Carbon by 2040 — Where We Stand
arguments.body:
Ten Years Ahead of the Paris Agreement

In 2019, Amazon co-founded The Climate Pledge — a commitment to reach net-zero carbon emissions across all operations by 2040, ten years ahead of the Paris Agreement's 2050 target. That commitment now has more than 400 signatories from companies across the globe who have made the same pledge. Amazon's role is both founder and benchmark: what Amazon does on sustainability, at Amazon's scale, matters in ways that most corporate sustainability commitments do not.

This article summarises where we stand today across our three major sustainability pillars: renewable energy, electric delivery, and packaging.

Pillar 1: Renewable Energy

Amazon is the world's largest corporate purchaser of renewable energy, with more than 100 gigawatts of contracted renewable energy capacity across solar and wind projects in the US, Europe, India, Australia, and beyond.

Key data points:

  • Amazon matched 100% of its electricity consumption with renewable energy for the first time in 2023 — a major milestone
  • Amazon has invested in over 400 renewable energy projects across 24 countries
  • The company is developing wind and solar projects that will power not just Amazon's operations but feed into local electricity grids, creating clean energy access beyond Amazon's footprint

Pillar 2: Electric Delivery Vehicles

In 2020, Amazon ordered 100,000 custom electric delivery vehicles from Rivian — the largest electric vehicle procurement order in history at the time. The first Rivian delivery vans entered service in 2022 and are now operating in multiple US cities.

  • Vehicles on road: Thousands of Rivian EDVs (Electric Delivery Vans) now operational in US cities including Seattle, Nashville, and Phoenix
  • Full deployment target: 100,000 Rivian vans on road by 2030
  • Supplementary EV investment: Additional electric vans from Mercedes (Europe), Stellantis, and other manufacturers supplement the Rivian order in international markets

Pillar 3: Packaging

Amazon's frustration-free packaging programme has been running since 2008. The latest targets:

  • Single-use plastic: Eliminated from delivery packaging in most markets; replacement with paper-based materials ongoing
  • Right-sized packaging: Machine-learning-driven packaging selection reduces box size and eliminates void fill in millions of shipments per day
  • Shipment Zero goal: Amazon's commitment to make 50% of all shipments net-zero carbon

The Climate Pledge Fund

Amazon has committed $2 billion to The Climate Pledge Fund — a venture capital initiative that invests in companies developing clean technologies that will help Amazon and other signatories meet their climate commitments. Investments span renewable energy, sustainable agriculture, circular economy, and carbon capture.

"The Climate Pledge is a race to zero. We founded it because we believe in the urgency, and because Amazon's scale means our progress — or our failure — matters at a global level." — Amazon Sustainability

Read the full Amazon Sustainability Report at sustainability.aboutamazon.com. For internal sustainability initiatives and local office programmes, visit A to Z > Sustainability Hub.

arguments.title: Rivian EDVs Hit the Road: Amazon's Electric Delivery Fleet Is Growing Every Quarter
arguments.body:
The Largest EV Order in History — Now Delivering to Your Neighbours

Amazon's order of 100,000 Rivian Electric Delivery Vans (EDVs) is the largest electric vehicle order ever placed by a single company. The first vehicles entered service in 2022, and as of 2024 they are operating in a growing number of US cities, with international electric delivery vehicles deployed in Europe and other markets.

These are not test pilots. The Rivian vans are operational delivery vehicles completing full routes every day — carrying Amazon packages to customers' doors with zero tailpipe emissions. Each van replaced a diesel or petrol vehicle that was previously making the same route.

The Vehicles

Rivian built the EDV specifically for Amazon, in two configurations: EDV 700 (for standard residential routes) and EDV 500 (for urban and high-density areas). The vans feature:

  • Purpose-built driver experience: ergonomic cab, 360-degree camera system, Alexa integration for route guidance and package lookup
  • Range: 150+ miles per charge — sufficient for a full delivery shift in most markets
  • Fleet management integration: real-time monitoring via Amazon's logistics platform for battery health, route optimisation, and maintenance scheduling
  • Safety technology: automatic emergency braking, pedestrian detection, and side-view cameras to protect delivery associates and road users

Cities Currently Operating Rivian EDVs

Rivian EDVs are now delivering Amazon packages in over a dozen US cities, including:

  • Seattle, Washington (home city — a fitting starting point)
  • Nashville, Tennessee
  • Phoenix, Arizona
  • San Diego, California
  • Denver, Colorado
  • Baltimore, Maryland

Additional city deployments are planned each quarter as Rivian's Bloomington, Illinois manufacturing plant scales production.

International: Electric Vans in Europe

In Europe, Amazon is deploying electric delivery vans from Mercedes-Benz (eSprinter) and Stellantis (Citroën ë-Dispatch and Peugeot e-Partner) across delivery stations in the UK, Germany, France, and Spain. The European rollout is accelerated by urban clean air zones (like the London ULEZ) that impose surcharges on diesel vehicles — making the economic case for electrification immediate, not just aspirational.

Charging Infrastructure

Amazon is building out EV charging infrastructure at its delivery stations across the US. Each station is being fitted with high-power charging equipment capable of fully charging multiple vans overnight. Amazon is working with utility companies on demand management to ensure charging loads are absorbed during off-peak grid periods, minimising carbon intensity of the charging itself.

"Every Rivian van that goes out on a route is a diesel van that didn't. At scale, that's millions of tonnes of CO₂ not emitted over the life of the fleet." — Amazon Last Mile Sustainability team

Track Amazon's EV progress in the annual Sustainability Report at sustainability.aboutamazon.com.

arguments.title: Prime Air Drone Delivery Is Live: What Amazonians Need to Know About Our Drone Programme
arguments.body:
Commercial Drone Delivery: No Longer a Future Project

Amazon Prime Air — the drone delivery programme that has been in development since 2013 — is now commercially operational in two US markets: College Station, Texas and Lockeford, California. Eligible customers in these areas can receive packages weighing up to five pounds delivered by drone in under an hour, with no delivery fee for Prime members.

This is not a demonstration or a limited trial. It is live commercial delivery service. And it represents a decade of engineering investment, regulatory navigation, and iteration on drone design, safety systems, and operating procedures reaching its first real-world proof of concept.

How Prime Air Works

The Prime Air delivery sequence works as follows:

  1. Customer places an eligible Prime Air order via the Amazon app — items are flagged as Prime Air eligible at checkout
  2. The order is picked and packed at a dedicated Prime Air fulfilment facility located close to the delivery zone
  3. The drone autonomously navigates to the delivery address, descends to approximately 12 feet above ground, and uses a cable to lower the package before releasing and ascending
  4. The entire process — from order to delivery — targets under 60 minutes

The MK30 Drone

The current Prime Air drone, the MK30, is Amazon's latest design — smaller, lighter, and quieter than previous models. It is designed to operate safely in residential neighbourhoods and can fly through light rain. Each drone is fully electric, producing zero direct emissions during delivery. Safety systems include redundant motors, autonomous obstacle detection, and a parachute-style failure mode that brings the drone to a controlled descent if primary systems fail.

Regulatory Progress

The FAA has granted Amazon an Air Carrier Certificate and Part 135 air carrier designation — the same certification required for commercial airlines. This was a significant regulatory milestone that unlocked commercial operations beyond restricted test zones. Amazon is working with the FAA on expanded certification to fly Beyond Visual Line of Sight (BVLOS) in more markets.

  • Current operational markets: College Station, TX; Lockeford, CA
  • Planned expansion markets: Additional US cities pending FAA approval
  • International markets: Regulatory engagement underway in the UK and select EU countries

Why This Matters Beyond the Headlines

Prime Air is not primarily about speed — same-day delivery already solves the speed problem in most markets. It is about last-mile cost reduction and zero-emission delivery at scale. A drone delivering five pounds of goods uses a fraction of the energy of a diesel van completing a route with the same package in it. If regulatory approval scales fast enough to deploy drones at meaningful volume, the impact on Amazon's delivery cost structure and carbon footprint is material.

"We've been building toward this for over a decade. The regulatory path was always the long pole. We're now flying commercially and expanding from here." — Amazon Prime Air team

Learn more about Prime Air at aboutamazon.com and follow expansion announcements on the Amazon Newsroom.

arguments.title: Amazon's Global Data Privacy Policy: Your Obligations Under GDPR, CCPA, and Data Residency Requirements
arguments.body:
Data Privacy Is a Legal Requirement and an Ethical Obligation

Amazon operates in more than 20 countries and processes personal data at a scale that is genuinely difficult to comprehend — customer data, employee data, partner data, and data generated by the operation of our services. This creates a legal obligation that every Amazonian who touches, processes, stores, or transfers personal data must understand and comply with.

This policy document is mandatory reading for all employees in roles that involve personal data handling, including product, engineering, operations, HR, legal, finance, and customer service. It is also relevant to any employee who accesses customer records, employee records, or partner information as part of their role.

GDPR: European Data Obligations

The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) applies to all processing of personal data of individuals in the European Union and European Economic Area, regardless of where Amazon's processing infrastructure is located. Key obligations:

  • Lawful basis: Every processing activity must have a documented lawful basis (consent, contract, legitimate interest, legal obligation, vital interests, or public task)
  • Data minimisation: Collect only what is necessary. Do not retain data beyond the period required for its stated purpose
  • Individual rights: EU residents have rights of access, correction, erasure ("right to be forgotten"), restriction, portability, and objection. These must be honoured within 30 days of a valid request
  • Data transfers: Transferring personal data from the EU to countries outside the EEA requires a legal transfer mechanism (Standard Contractual Clauses, adequacy decision, or binding corporate rules)
  • Breach notification: Data breaches must be reported to the relevant supervisory authority within 72 hours of discovery. Report immediately via the Security Hub on A to Z

Amazon received a €746 million GDPR fine from Luxembourg's CNPD in 2021. Compliance is not theoretical — regulators act.

CCPA: California Consumer Privacy Act

The California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), as amended by the California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA), gives California residents rights over their personal information, including the right to know what data is collected, the right to delete, and the right to opt out of the sale of personal information. If your role involves consumer-facing data operations in the US, CCPA compliance is mandatory.

AWS Data Residency and Sovereignty

AWS customers in many countries require guarantees that their data remains within specific geographic boundaries. Amazon's obligations as both an AWS operator and an AWS customer include:

  • Understanding data residency requirements in each market before deploying services
  • Using AWS Region selection to ensure data residency for customer workloads
  • Complying with national data localisation laws (e.g., Germany's BDSG, India's DPDP Act, Saudi Arabia's PDPL)

Your Responsibilities

Every Amazonian is responsible for:

  • Completing the mandatory annual Data Privacy Training module on A to Z
  • Not accessing personal data beyond what is required for your specific role
  • Reporting suspected data breaches, loss of devices, or unauthorised access immediately via the Security Hub
  • Consulting your Legal Business Partner before building any new system or process that involves personal data collection or processing
"Privacy by design is not a technical checklist. It is a mindset. Build systems that collect the minimum necessary data, protect it appropriately, and make it easy for customers and employees to exercise their rights." — Amazon Legal & Privacy

Questions: Contact your Legal Business Partner or submit via A to Z > Legal > Privacy Enquiry.

arguments.title: Amazon's Antitrust Environment: What the FTC Lawsuit Means for Amazonians
arguments.body:
What You Need to Know — and What You Should Not Speculate About

In September 2023, the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) filed a lawsuit against Amazon alleging that Amazon illegally maintains monopoly power in online retail and online marketplace services. This is one of the most significant antitrust cases involving a technology company in the United States in a generation. Trial is expected in 2026.

This article is the authoritative internal briefing on this matter. It will be updated as material developments occur. Please read it carefully — and please follow the guidelines at the bottom regarding communications and documentation.

The Nature of the Allegations

The FTC's complaint focuses on two claims:

  1. Anti-competitive conduct in the online retail market: The FTC alleges that Amazon uses various practices — including pricing parity enforcement and Prime eligibility conditions — that harm third-party sellers and ultimately consumers by suppressing competition from rival retail platforms
  2. Anti-competitive conduct in the online marketplace services market: The FTC alleges that Amazon's fulfilment requirements and platform rules foreclose competition in the marketplace services space

Amazon disputes these allegations in their entirety. Amazon's legal position is that its practices are pro-competitive, have led to lower prices and better selection for customers, and have created economic opportunity for hundreds of thousands of small and medium-sized businesses who sell through the Amazon store.

Additional Regulatory Proceedings

In parallel with the FTC matter, Amazon faces additional regulatory scrutiny:

  • EU Digital Markets Act: Amazon has been designated as a "gatekeeper" under the DMA, which imposes specific interoperability, data access, and fairness obligations on how Amazon operates its marketplace in Europe
  • UK CMA: The UK Competition and Markets Authority has ongoing investigations into Amazon's marketplace practices
  • NLRB proceedings: The National Labor Relations Board has ongoing proceedings related to worker organising and labour practices at Amazon facilities

Your Responsibilities During This Period

Active legal proceedings create specific obligations for all Amazon employees. These are not optional:

  • Litigation Hold: If you have received a litigation hold notice, you must preserve all relevant documents, emails, and records as directed. Destruction of documents under a hold notice has severe legal consequences
  • Do not speculate in writing: Do not write emails, Slack/Chime messages, or documents that speculate about Amazon's legal position, liability, or settlement prospects. Written internal speculation can be discoverable
  • Do not discuss with external parties: Do not discuss the litigation with journalists, analysts, social media followers, or any non-Amazon parties. Refer all press enquiries to press@amazon.com
  • Contact Legal first: If you are contacted by the FTC, another regulator, or any third party in connection with these proceedings, contact your Legal Business Partner immediately before responding
"Amazon will contest these allegations vigorously. We are confident in our legal position. In the meantime, every employee should follow the document preservation and communications protocols above — not because we have anything to hide, but because legal discipline is how you defend a strong position effectively." — David Zapolsky, Chief Legal Officer

Questions about the litigation hold or your specific obligations: contact your Legal Business Partner via A to Z > Legal > My Legal Team.

arguments.title: EthicsLine: How to Report Concerns Anonymously — and Why It Matters
arguments.body:
Amazon's EthicsLine: Your Anonymous Reporting Channel

Amazon's EthicsLine is the company's confidential and anonymous reporting system for ethics, compliance, legal, and safety concerns. It is managed by an independent third-party provider — not Amazon employees — which means reports can be made anonymously with genuine confidentiality. Amazon's policy strictly prohibits retaliation against anyone who makes a good-faith report.

This article explains what EthicsLine is for, how to use it, and what happens after you submit a report.

What You Can Report

EthicsLine is the right channel for a wide range of concerns, including but not limited to:

  • Financial misconduct: fraud, falsification of records, misuse of company funds, expense fraud
  • Anti-competitive behaviour: coordination with competitors, price-fixing, market allocation
  • Privacy violations: unauthorised access to customer or employee data, improper data sharing
  • Harassment and discrimination: conduct that violates Amazon's Equal Employment Opportunity policy or anti-harassment standards
  • Safety violations: unsafe working conditions in fulfilment centres, delivery stations, or offices (though safety concerns should also be logged via the Safety Hub in A to Z for immediate operational response)
  • Conflicts of interest: undisclosed outside business interests, insider trading, supplier kickbacks
  • Data and information security: suspected breaches, credential sharing, suspicious access patterns
  • Legal and regulatory violations: any conduct that you believe violates applicable law

What EthicsLine Is NOT For

EthicsLine is for ethics and compliance concerns — not for HR grievances, performance disputes, or general workplace complaints. For those matters, use A to Z > My HR > Raise a Concern, or speak with your HR Business Partner directly.

How to Submit a Report

  • Online: Via the EthicsLine portal (access via A to Z > Legal & Compliance > EthicsLine)
  • Phone: EthicsLine telephone numbers by country are listed on A to Z
  • Anonymous: You may submit a report without identifying yourself. The third-party provider ensures Amazon does not know the identity of anonymous reporters

What Happens After You Report

All EthicsLine reports are reviewed by Amazon's Global Ethics & Compliance team. The process:

  1. Report is received and triaged within 2 business days
  2. An investigator is assigned (independent of the team or subject of the report)
  3. Investigation is conducted. If you provided contact information, you will receive updates
  4. Findings are documented. Appropriate action is taken, which may include disciplinary measures, process changes, or regulatory reporting
  5. If your report identified a genuine compliance issue, you will be informed of the outcome to the extent permissible

Non-Retaliation Policy

Amazon's non-retaliation policy is absolute. Any Amazon employee, manager, or executive who retaliates against a person for making a good-faith EthicsLine report will face disciplinary action up to and including termination. If you believe you have experienced retaliation, report it through EthicsLine immediately.

"Speak up. The culture we want to build requires that people feel safe saying when something is wrong. EthicsLine is the mechanism. Use it." — Amazon Global Ethics & Compliance

arguments.title: Project Sequoia: The Next Generation of Fulfilment Centre Robotics Is Here
arguments.body:
What Is Project Sequoia?

Amazon's fulfilment centre robotics programme has been running since the acquisition of Kiva Systems in 2012 — now operating as Amazon Robotics. The hundreds of thousands of drive units moving pods of inventory across FC floors represent one of the largest deployments of autonomous mobile robots anywhere in the world. And we're about to leap forward again.

Project Sequoia is Amazon's next-generation robotics system for inventory management inside fulfilment centres. It represents a fundamental redesign of how inventory is received, stored, and retrieved — with the goal of reducing the time between an item arriving at an FC and being available for purchase by up to 75%.

How Sequoia Works

Sequoia integrates several robotic subsystems into a unified inventory workflow:

  • Automated receiving: Inbound inventory is sorted, identified, and stowed by robotic systems, reducing manual receiving time from hours to minutes per pallet
  • High-density storage: Sequoia uses a vertical storage system with tighter pod spacing and higher rack density than the current Kiva-era systems — more inventory per square foot, without expanding the building footprint
  • Integrated Sparrow system: The Sparrow robotic arm — capable of identifying and handling over 200 million different Amazon products — works within the Sequoia architecture to pick individual items from pods and transfer them to packing stations
  • Project Titan compatibility: For heavy items, Project Titan autonomous mobile robots (capable of handling pallets and large items) are being integrated into the Sequoia workflow

Associate Impact

Sequoia changes the nature of FC associate roles. The most physically demanding tasks — walking long distances to retrieve inventory, lifting heavy items from low or high storage positions — are handled by robotic systems. Associates work at ergonomic workstations where items come to them, rather than walking miles per shift to retrieve them.

This is a point of emphasis for Amazon's operations leadership: automation at Amazon is designed to reduce the most physically demanding and injury-prone elements of FC work, while creating new roles in robotics maintenance, fleet management, and quality assurance. Amazon Technical Academy provides a pathway for operations associates to move into technical roles as robotics density increases.

Scale of Deployment

Sequoia is being deployed at selected fulfilment centres in the US first, with rollout plans extending to international FCs. The first Sequoia-enabled facility began operations in 2023, and the learnings from early deployment are informing the next phase of rollout across Amazon's 350+ global fulfilment network.

"Sequoia isn't about replacing people. It's about redesigning how the work gets done so that it's safer, faster, and less physically punishing. That matters if you want to be Earth's Best Employer." — Amazon Robotics & Operations leadership

Associates with questions about automation in their facility should speak with their Operations Manager or visit the Robotics Hub on A to Z.

arguments.title: Amazon Logistics: How We Went from 0% to 70% of Our Own US Deliveries in a Decade
arguments.body:
The Last-Mile Revolution That Changed Amazon's Cost Structure

In 2012, Amazon relied almost entirely on UPS, FedEx, and USPS to deliver its packages. Today, Amazon Logistics handles approximately 70% of Amazon's US deliveries — a shift that is one of the most significant strategic transformations in the company's history, and one of the most underappreciated from the outside.

Understanding the Amazon last-mile delivery network — its components, its economics, and where it is going — is essential context for anyone working in operations, supply chain, transportation, or related roles.

The Four Layers of Amazon's Delivery Network

1. Delivery Service Partners (DSPs)

Amazon's DSP programme allows entrepreneurs to start small delivery businesses with Amazon as their primary customer. Amazon provides vans (including Rivian EDVs), a technology platform, and package volume. DSPs hire drivers, manage operations, and are paid per package delivered. There are now thousands of DSPs operating across the US, each running fleets of 20 to 40 vans. This model gives Amazon control over the last mile without the direct employment costs and labour relations complexity of a unionised delivery workforce.

2. Amazon Flex

Amazon Flex enables individuals to make deliveries using their own vehicles — similar in concept to Uber's model. Flex drivers are independent contractors who pick up packages from delivery stations and deliver them in their personal vehicles. Flex is used primarily for surge capacity, rural areas where DSP density is low, and same-day delivery programmes where small batches of packages need to move quickly.

3. Amazon Air

Amazon Air is the company's cargo aircraft network, operating a fleet of dedicated Amazon-branded aircraft from regional air hubs. Amazon Air moves packages between Amazon's sort centres and regional delivery stations overnight — enabling next-day and same-day delivery commitments in markets that are too far from fulfilment centres for ground transport to meet time windows. Key Amazon Air hubs include Cincinnati/Northern Kentucky Airport (the primary hub), Denver, and Dallas Fort Worth.

4. Amazon Robotics — Delivery Stations

Amazon's network of delivery stations — the final link before packages go on vans — is now increasingly automated. Automated sortation systems read package labels, route them to the correct DSP bag, and significantly reduce the manual labour involved in station sorting. This reduces cost-per-package at the station level and reduces the error rate in misrouted packages.

The Economics of Owning the Last Mile

The decision to build Amazon Logistics was ultimately an economic one. At Amazon's volume, third-party carrier rates — even with the negotiating leverage of Amazon's volume — were increasing faster than Amazon's ability to absorb the cost within its Prime commitments. Building the last mile in-house allowed Amazon to:

  • Control delivery speed and quality without dependence on external carriers
  • Reduce cost-per-delivery below external carrier rates at scale
  • Electrify the fleet directly (the Rivian order is only possible because Amazon owns the fleet)
  • Generate data about delivery performance that could be fed back into FC and sortation operations
"We didn't build Amazon Logistics to disrupt the parcel industry. We built it because it was the only way to make our delivery promises economics work at the scale we were growing to." — Amazon Transportation leadership

arguments.title: Workplace Safety in Amazon Fulfilment: OSHA Compliance, Recordables, and What Every Associate Needs to Know
arguments.body:
Safety Is Non-Negotiable

Amazon operates one of the world's largest industrial workforces — approximately 1.15 million people in operations, fulfilment, and logistics roles globally. The safety of every associate, driver, and contractor in that workforce is a direct operational responsibility, not a compliance checkbox. This article covers what every operations associate and manager needs to know about Amazon's safety obligations, OSHA requirements, and how to report concerns.

OSHA Compliance: The Legal Framework

In the United States, Amazon's fulfilment centres and delivery stations are regulated by the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA). OSHA requires employers to:

  • Maintain a workplace free from recognised hazards that are causing or are likely to cause serious physical harm or death
  • Record and report work-related injuries and illnesses that meet the OSHA recordable criteria
  • Allow OSHA inspectors access to the workplace and records
  • Post the OSHA "It's The Law" poster in a prominent location in every facility
  • Provide required safety training in a language and vocabulary that workers can understand

International operations are subject to equivalent regulations: HSE (UK), DGUV (Germany), WorkSafe (Australia), and national equivalents elsewhere. The obligations are broadly similar even where the regulatory body differs.

Amazon's Safety Programmes

Ergonomics and Injury Prevention

Musculoskeletal injuries — strains, sprains, and overexertion — are the most common work-related injuries in fulfilment operations. Amazon's primary prevention programme focuses on:

  • Workstation design: Adjustable heights, reduced reach distances, and ergonomic tool selection reduce injury risk at the process level
  • Task rotation: Rotating associates through different task types limits cumulative strain on specific muscle groups
  • WorkingWell: Amazon's investment programme for physical and mental health in operations, including on-site physical therapists, AmaZen mindfulness stations, and strength-building equipment in break rooms

Reporting Safety Concerns

Every safety concern must be reported. There are no consequences for reporting a hazard — there are consequences for ignoring one. Report via:

  • A to Z Safety Hub: Log any safety hazard, near-miss, or unsafe condition digitally in real time
  • Your Area Manager or Safety Team: For immediate risks, alert your manager or your site's Safety team directly and immediately
  • EthicsLine: If you believe a safety concern is being ignored or suppressed, you can report anonymously via EthicsLine

Recordable vs. Non-Recordable Incidents

Not every incident is OSHA-recordable. An incident is recordable if it results in days away from work, restricted work or job transfer, medical treatment beyond first aid, loss of consciousness, or a significant diagnosis. Near-misses — incidents that could have resulted in injury but did not — are not recordable under OSHA but are strongly encouraged to be reported internally as part of Amazon's proactive safety culture.

"The goal isn't zero recordables on a spreadsheet. The goal is zero injuries in the real world. That requires every person on every shift to report every hazard, every time — no exceptions." — Amazon Global Safety leadership

Your facility's injury and illness logs are available from your site's Safety Manager. Amazon publishes aggregate safety data in its annual Sustainability Report.
